html:
XV

Olivier Visits the Comte de Passavant

вАЬI was afraid your brother hadnвАЩt delivered my message,вАЭ said Robert on seeing Olivier come into the room.

вАЬAm I late?вАЭ he asked, coming forward timidly and almost on tiptoe. He had kept his hat in his hand and Robert took it from him.

вАЬPut that down. Make yourself comfortable. Here, in this armchair, I think youвАЩll be all right. Not late at all, to judge by the clock. But my wish to see you went faster than the time. Do you smoke?вАЭ

вАЬNo, thank you,вАЭ said Olivier, waving aside the cigarette case, which the Comte de Passavant held out to him. He refused out of shyness, though he was really longing to try one of the slender, amber-scented cigarettes (Russian, no doubt,) which lay ranged in the proffered case.

вАЬYes, IвАЩm glad you were able to come. I was afraid you might be too much taken up with your examination. When is it?вАЭ

вАЬThe written is in ten days. But IвАЩm not working much. I think IвАЩm ready and IвАЩm more afraid of being fagged when I go up.вАЭ

вАЬStill, I suppose youвАЩd refuse to undertake any other occupation just now?вАЭ

вАЬNoвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ if it isnвАЩt too absorbing, that is.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩll tell you why I asked you to come. First, for the pleasure of seeing you again. The other night in the foyer, during the entrвАЩacte, we were just getting into a talk. I was exceedingly interested by what you said. I expect you donвАЩt remember?вАЭ

вАЬOh yes, I do,вАЭ said Olivier, who was under the impression he had said nothing but stupidities.

вАЬBut today I have something special to say to you.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I think you know an individual of the Hebrew persuasion, called Dhurmer? IsnвАЩt he one of your schoolfellows?вАЭ

вАЬI have just this moment left him.вАЭ

вАЬAh! You see a good deal of each other?вАЭ

вАЬYes. We met at the Louvre today to talk about a review of which he is to be the editor.вАЭ

Robert burst into a loud, affected laugh.

вАЬHa! Ha! Ha! the editor!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ HeвАЩs in a deuce of a hurry.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Did he really say that to you?вАЭ

вАЬHe has been talking to me about it for ever so long.вАЭ

вАЬYes. I have been thinking of it for some time past. The other day I asked him casually whether heвАЩd agree to read over the manuscripts with me; thatвАЩs what he at once called becoming editorвБ†вАФnot even subeditor; I didnвАЩt contradict him and he immediately.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Just like him, isnвАЩt it? What a fellow! He wants taking down a peg or two.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ DonвАЩt you really smoke?вАЭ

вАЬAfter all, I think I will,вАЭ said Olivier, this time accepting. вАЬThank you.вАЭ

вАЬWell, allow me to say, OlivierвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ you donвАЩt mind my calling you Olivier, do you? I really canвАЩt say Monsieur; youвАЩre too young, and IвАЩm too intimate with your brother Vincent to call you Molinier. Well then, Olivier, allow me to say that I have infinitely more confidence in your taste than in Mr.¬†Solomon DhurmerвАЩs. Now would you consent to taking the literary direction? Under me a little, of courseвБ†вАФat first, at any rate. But I prefer not to have my name on the cover. IвАЩll tell you why later.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Perhaps youвАЩd take a glass of port wine, eh? IвАЩve got some thatвАЩs quite good.вАЭ

He stretched out his hand to a kind of little sideboard that stood near and took up a bottle of wine and two glasses, which he filled.

вАЬWell! What do you think?вАЭ

вАЬYes, indeed; first-rate.вАЭ

вАЬI wasnвАЩt talking of the port,вАЭ protested Robert, laughing; вАЬbut of what I was saying just now.вАЭ

Olivier had pretended not to understand. He was afraid of accepting too quickly and of showing his joy too obviously. He blushed a little and stammered with confusion:

вАЬMy examination wouldnвАЩt.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬYou have just told me that you werenвАЩt giving much time to it,вАЭ interrupted Robert. вАЬAnd besides, the review wonвАЩt come out yet awhile. I am wondering whether it wouldnвАЩt be better to put off launching it till after the holidays. But in any case I had to sound you. We must get several numbers ready before October and we ought to see each other a great deal this summer so as to talk things over. What are you going to do these holidays?вАЭ

вАЬI donвАЩt know exactly. My people will probably be going to Normandy. They always do in the summer.вАЭ

вАЬAnd you will have to go with them?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ CouldnвАЩt you let yourself be unhitched for a bit?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬMy mother would never consent.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩm dining tonight with your brother. May I speak to him about it?вАЭ

вАЬOh, Vincent wonвАЩt be with us.вАЭ Then, realizing that this sentence was no answer to the question, he added: вАЬBesides, it wouldnвАЩt do any good.вАЭ

вАЬWell, but if we find a good reason to give Mamma?вАЭ

Olivier did not answer. He loved his mother tenderly and the mocking tone in which Robert alluded to her displeased him. Robert understood that he had gone too far.

вАЬSo you appreciate my port,вАЭ he said by way of diversion. вАЬHave another glass?вАЭ

вАЬNo, no, thank you; but itвАЩs excellent.вАЭ

вАЬYes, I was struck by the ripeness and sureness of your judgment the other night. Do you mean to go in for criticism?вАЭ

вАЬNo.вАЭ

вАЬPoetry?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I know you write poetry.вАЭ

Olivier blushed again.

вАЬYes, your brother has betrayed you. And no doubt you know other young men who would be ready to contribute. This review must become a rallying ground for the younger generation. ThatвАЩs its raison dвАЩ√™tre. I should like you to help me draw up a kind of prospectus, a manifesto, which would just give a sketch of the new tendencies without defining them too precisely. WeвАЩll talk it over later on. We must make a choice of two or three telling epithets; they mustnвАЩt be neologisms; no old words that are thoroughly hackneyed; weвАЩll fill them with a brand new meaning and make the public swallow them. After Flaubert there was вАШcadenced and rhythmicвАЩ; after Leconte de Lisle, вАШhieratic and definitiveвАЩ.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Oh! what would you say to вАШvital,вАЩ eh?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАШUnconscious and vitalвАЩ.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ No?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАШElementary, unconscious and vitalвАЩ?вАЭ

вАЬI think we might find something better still,вАЭ Olivier took courage to say, smiling, though without seeming to approve much.

вАЬCome, another glass of port.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬNot quite full, please.вАЭ

вАЬYou see, the great weakness of the symbolist school is that it brought nothing but an aesthetic with it; all the other great schools brought with them, besides their new styles, a new ethic, new tables, a new way of looking at things, of understanding love, of behaving oneself in life. As for the symbolist, itвАЩs perfectly simple; he didnвАЩt behave himself at all in life; he didnвАЩt attempt to understand it; he denied its existence; he turned his back on it. Absurd, donвАЩt you think? They were a set of people without greedвБ†вАФwithout appetites even. Not like usвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ eh?вАЭ

Olivier had finished his second glass of port and his second cigarette. Reclining in his comfortable armchair, with his eyes half shut, he said nothing, but signified his assent by slightly nodding his head from time to time. At this moment a ring was heard, and almost immediately afterwards a servant entered with a card which he presented to Robert. Robert took the card, glanced at it and put it on his writing desk beside him.

вАЬVery well. Ask him to wait a moment.вАЭ The servant went out. вАЬLook here, my dear boy, I like you very much and I think we shall get on very well together. But somebody has just come whom I absolutely must see and he wants to speak to me alone.вАЭ

Olivier had risen.

вАЬIвАЩll show you out by the garden, if youвАЩll allow me.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Ah! whilst I think of it. Would you care to have my new book? IвАЩve got a copy here, on handmade paper.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬI havenвАЩt waited for that to read it,вАЭ said Olivier, who didnвАЩt much care for PassavantвАЩs book, and tried his best to be amiable without being fulsome.

Did Passavant detect in his tone a certain tincture of disdain? He went on quickly: вАЬOh, you neednвАЩt say anything about it. If you were to tell me you liked it, I should be obliged to doubt either your taste or your sincerity. No; no one knows better than I do whatвАЩs lacking in the book. I wrote it much too quickly. To tell the truth, the whole time I was writing it I was thinking of my next one. Ah! that one is a different matter. I care about that one. Yes, I care about it exceedingly. YouвАЩll see; youвАЩll see.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ IвАЩm so very sorry, but you really must leave me now.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Unless.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ No, no; we donвАЩt know each other well enough yet, and your people are certainly expecting you back for dinner. Well, goodbye; au revoir. IвАЩll write your name in the book; allow me.вАЭ

He had risen; he went up to his writing desk. While he was stooping to write, Olivier stepped forward and glanced out of the corner of his eye at the card which the servant had just brought in:

Victor Strouvilhou

The name meant nothing to him.

Passavant handed Olivier the copy of The Horizontal Bar, and as Olivier was preparing to read the inscription:

вАЬLook at it later,вАЭ said Passavant, slipping the book under his arm.

It was not till he was in the street that Olivier read the manuscript motto with which the Comte de Passavant had adorned the first page and which he had culled out of the book itself:

вАЬPrithee, Orlando, a few steps further. I am not perfectly sure that I dare altogether take your meaning.вАЭ

Underneath which he had added:

To Olivier Molinier
from his presumptive friend
Comte Robert de Passavant

An ambiguous motto, which made Olivier wonder, but which after all he was perfectly free to interpret as he pleased.

Olivier got home just after Edouard had left, weary of waiting.
